Title: Govt: No blanket freeze on 2026 allocations for Health, Education ministries
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government is not imposing a blanket freeze on 2026 allocations for the Health Ministry and Education Ministry.
The Finance Ministry said that for the Health Ministry, the government's priority remained the delivery of public healthcare services, maintenance of health facilities, procurement of medicines and medical equipment, and strengthening the capacity of the national healthcare system.
"Any expenditure adjustments are made without compromising core services to the public," the ministry said in a written reply published on the parliament website today.
The reply was in response to a question from Datuk Seri Mohd Shafie Apdal (Warisan-Semporna) on whether allocations for the Health Ministry and Education Ministry this year had been reduced.
The Finance Ministry said the government also continued to prioritise school operations, the welfare of students and teachers, maintenance of educational institutions, and the implementation of high-impact education programmes.
"The measures implemented to optimise spending focus on areas where savings can be achieved without compromising the quality of, or access to, education," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Union warns of 'conflict' as Volkswagen eyes mass job cuts
Article:
GERMANY: Volkswagen workers staged protests nationwide on Thursday as unions warned of a "major conflict" if the struggling German car giant pushes ahead with what could be the global auto industry's biggest restructuring.
Europe's largest carmaker has come under intense pressure from US tariffs, slimmer profit margins from electric vehicles and, above all, intense competition in China, the world's largest auto market.
Thousands of job cuts have already been announced, but reports said chief executive officer Oliver Blume is considering increasing them to 100,000 and potentially closing four factories in Germany.
As Volkswagen's management presented its planned overhaul to the 10-brand group's supervisory board, workers staged protests outside plants and unions warned they were ready to step up industrial action.
"Whoever takes on the workers is risking a major conflict," Thorsten Groeger, an official from union IG Metall, told reporters at Volkswagen's headquarters in Wolfsburg.
"We will not stand by and do nothing if the company does not change course."
At one of the factories reportedly earmarked for closure in the eastern German city of Zwickau, about 200 workers joined a demonstration, including one dressed as the Grim Reaper.
"This site will not be closed, not against our will. We will defend it," union official Thomas Knabel told the crowd, who waved banners reading: "United, fighting for our future".
**'Dramatic situation'**
"If the plant were to be shut down, it would be the death blow for the region," Denise Tschiersch, who has worked at the factory since 1997 and sits on its works council, told AFP.
"This really is a dramatic situation."
Volkswagen confirmed late on Thursday that it had presented its plans to the supervisory board, comprising a package of 12 measures.
These include reducing production capacity to nine million vehicles a year from 10 million currently, and cutting its model range by up to 50 per cent, Volkswagen said in a statement.
It made no mention, however, of potential job cuts or factory closures.
"We are making the Volkswagen Group faster, more robust and more competitive," Blume said.
Volkswagen's works council, an elected body representing staff interests, accused management of failing to provide sufficient information to employees and demanded that Blume "comment unambiguously" on Friday on speculation about job cuts.
"The way the board is treating the workforce is the height of disrespect," works council chief Daniela Cavallo said.
**Worsening outlook**
Volkswagen, whose brands range from mass market SEATs to premium Porsches, had previously announced plans to cut up to 50,000 jobs in Germany, including 35,000 at its flagship Volkswagen brand.
However, the outlook has since worsened considerably, prompting management to seek far deeper cuts and factory closures.
Management is also reportedly seeking to spin off the Volkswagen brand as a separate company, which unions fear is an attempt to undermine existing protections for employees.
If the new plans are implemented, they would amount to a roughly 15 per cent reduction in Volkswagen's global workforce and eclipse all other major job-cutting programmes in the automotive industry.
This includes Detroit-based General Motors' move to cut almost 50,000 jobs in 2009 after filing for bankruptcy.
Germany's automotive industry, including Volkswagen's peers BMW and Mercedes-Benz and their suppliers, has been struggling in recent years, with job cuts and restructuring becoming increasingly common.
Challenges in key international markets have intensified the pressure on Volkswagen.
US President Donald Trump's tariff measures are costing Volkswagen billions of euros a year, while the carmaker is losing ground in China to local rivals, particularly in electric vehicles.
However, pushing through such a sweeping overhaul at the 89-year-old group could prove difficult.
Volkswagen has a complex ownership structure, with the state of Lower Saxony, home to Wolfsburg and six Volkswagen plants, holding a substantial stake that gives it the power to block decisions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Banting school stabbing suspect arrives at court
Article:
BANTING: The 15-year-old girl accused of stabbing a schoolmate at a secondary school has been brought to the Telok Datuk magistrate's court here.
A police vehicle carrying the suspect, who was seated in the back seat, entered the court complex at 8.33am.
Dressed in an all-black outfit with a hood over her head, the juvenile was escorted into the courtroom by several police officers.
Tight security was maintained at the court gate, with the media barred from the compound and entry restricted to those with official court matters.
As the suspect is a minor and her identity must be protected, the charge proceedings will be held behind closed doors, with only relevant parties allowed to attend.
It is understood that she will appear before magistrate Maslinda Selamat.
The girl's four-day remand order expires today.
The victim sustained injuries to several parts of her body, including her lungs, in the knife attack, which occurred at about 9.30am on Monday and is currently receiving treatment in hospital.
The suspect had reportedly been absent from school for eight months and was studying from home due to health issues while undergoing medical treatment.
Selangor police chief Datuk Shazeli Kahar had said that
initial investigations showed
the suspect had slipped into the school after noticing the gate was left open.
He said the suspect had the weapon inside a bag she was carrying.
On the suspect's family background, Shazeli said the teenager was believed to be suffering from emotional stress and depression.
He confirmed a person with disabilities card under the autism category registered in the suspect's name.
He added that she was also involved in an incident where she fell from a school building late last year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: #NSTviral: Motorists face tense elephant encounter on Gerik trunk road [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A lone elephant brought traffic to a standstill along the Gerik trunk road after approaching several vehicles in a viral video showing a tense wildlife encounter.
The video, posted on TikTok by user @iman.shajaya, is believed to have been recorded by a woman travelling in a vehicle behind the incident on July 3 at 3.48pm.
The footage shows the elephant, towering over the vehicles, calmly emerging onto the road, apparently separated from its herd, before stopping traffic.
It first approached a white Toyota Alphard, slowly walking around the multi-purpose vehicle from the right before making its way to the left side as though carefully inspecting it.
Behind the Alphard was a large-capacity motorcycle ridden by a man dressed entirely in black, while another car waited behind the SUV.
After finishing its "inspection" of the Alphard, the elephant appeared to notice the motorcyclist and took a few slow steps towards him.
Faced with the massive wild animal, the rider cautiously reversed his motorcycle, keeping as much distance as possible while avoiding any sudden movements.
Just as it seemed the elephant might continue approaching him, it suddenly lost interest, walked past the Alphard and crossed to the opposite side of the road.
With the elephant safely out of their path, all three vehicles quickly drove away.
Throughout the tense encounter, the woman recording the video could be heard softly reciting verses of prayer as the elephant lingered among the vehicles.
The clip has since attracted thousands of views and comments, with many expressing relief that no one was injured.
Several users remarked that only prayers could calm nerves in such a situation.
"Only prayers came to mind. May Allah protect everyone," one user commented.
Another wrote, "Alhamdulillah... everyone was kept safe."
Others found humour in the elephant's seemingly methodical behaviour.
One user joked that the elephant was "checking out the cars before deciding which one to buy."
Another quipped, "It looked like it was conducting a JPJ inspection."
Many sympathised with the motorcyclist, saying they could only imagine how frightening it must have been to come face-to-face with an elephant that outweighed both him and his motorcycle many times over.
The Gerik-Jeli route, which cuts through forested areas, is known to be frequented by wild elephants, and motorists travelling along the stretch are regularly reminded to exercise caution, especially when animals are spotted near or on the road.

Title: When leaders must lead
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: One of the most frustrating realities of leadership is discovering that not everyone performs with the same level of commitment. Most teams have individuals who consistently step forward, while others seem to disappear whenever the pressure rises.
Deadlines approach, challenges emerge, and suddenly the reliable few are left carrying the burden for everyone else. It is an uncomfortable situation, but one every leader must be prepared to address.
Leadership is not about hoping people will do the right thing. It is about creating an environment where accountability is expected, recognised and upheld. Ignoring poor commitment rarely makes it disappear. In fact, it usually encourages it.
The first responsibility of a leader is to distinguish between someone who is unwilling and someone who is unable. A team member may appear absent because they lack confidence, are overwhelmed, or are dealing with personal difficulties. Another may simply have chosen not to engage. These situations require different responses. Compassion should never replace accountability, but accountability should never exclude compassion.
Clear expectations prevent many future problems. People should understand exactly what is expected of them, what success looks like, and what responsibilities they carry. Ambiguity gives people room to justify poor performance. Clarity removes excuses.
Leaders must also avoid making assumptions. Before drawing conclusions, have honest conversations. Ask questions rather than make accusations. Sometimes what appears to be laziness is actually confusion, poor communication or a lack of resources. Understanding the cause allows the leader to respond appropriately rather than emotionally.
However, repeated patterns cannot be ignored. When someone consistently disappears during critical moments, the issue is no longer about circumstances. It becomes a matter of character, commitment and reliability. Every team depends upon trust, and trust quickly erodes when people cannot rely on one another.
Nothing damages morale faster than allowing dependable people to carry the workload while others contribute very little. High performers notice everything. They notice who stays late, who volunteers, who delivers, and who quietly disappears when work becomes difficult. If leaders tolerate this imbalance, they unintentionally punish commitment while rewarding complacency.
Many leaders make the mistake of protecting harmony at the expense of standards. Difficult conversations are postponed because confrontation feels uncomfortable. Yet avoiding conflict rarely preserves harmony. It simply transfers frustration to those who continue doing the work.
Addressing poor commitment does not require aggression. It requires honesty. Calm, respectful conversations often have greater impact than emotional criticism. Describe the behaviour, explain its consequences on the wider team, and make expectations unmistakably clear moving forward.
Consistency is equally important. Rules that apply only to some people quickly lose credibility. When leaders excuse one person's behaviour while holding others accountable, they create resentment. Fairness is not treating everyone identically; it is applying standards consistently and transparently.
Leaders should also recognise and celebrate those who consistently step up. Too often attention focuses only on those causing problems, while dependable team members quietly continue delivering results without acknowledgement. Recognition reinforces the behaviours that strengthen teams.
At the same time, leaders must resist the temptation to rely exclusively on their strongest performers. The dependable few are often asked to solve every crisis because they can be trusted. Over time, this creates exhaustion and resentment. Reliable people are not an unlimited resource. Even the most committed individuals eventually become tired if they feel others are being carried indefinitely.
Building resilience means developing everyone, not just relying on the best. Leaders should coach, mentor and create opportunities for growth before a crisis arrives. Teams perform better when responsibility is shared rather than concentrated among a handful of individuals.
Culture also plays a decisive role. Teams quickly learn what behaviours are accepted. If disappearing during difficult periods carries no consequences, others may eventually follow the same example. Conversely, when commitment, ownership and reliability are consistently valued, these behaviours become part of the team's identity.
Leaders themselves must model the standards they expect. Nothing undermines credibility faster than asking others to make sacrifices that the leader is unwilling to make. During demanding periods, people watch their leaders closely. Presence matters. Visibility matters. Commitment from the top inspires commitment throughout the team.
Not every person will respond positively to feedback. Some will improve, while others will continue repeating the same behaviour despite support, coaching and clear expectations. Leaders must accept that not everyone belongs in every team. Protecting the culture sometimes requires making difficult decisions about those who consistently undermine it.
This is not about perfection. Every individual will have moments of struggle, make mistakes or require support. Strong teams allow room for humanity. What they cannot tolerate is repeated indifference disguised as temporary difficulty.
Ultimately, leadership is about protecting both people and purpose. That means supporting those who genuinely need help while challenging those who repeatedly avoid responsibility. It means recognising effort, confronting complacency and ensuring that reliability becomes the norm rather than the exception.
The strongest teams are not built because everyone is equally talented. They are built because everyone understands that when the critical moment arrives, every person shows up, contributes fully and can be counted upon. That is the culture great leaders create, protect and never compromise.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six South Koreans among 11 held in raid on World Cup betting syndicate
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Police arrested 11 people, including six South Korean nationals, after raiding a World Cup football betting call centre at an office building in Taman Desa, Brickfields here yesterday.
Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus said the 11.30am raid was conducted by the Gambling, Vice and Secret Societies Division of the Kuala Lumpur police headquarters Criminal Investigation Department.
He said police seized computer sets, electronic equipment, and cash, alongside discovering a digital currency balance of 323,380.22 USDT (about RM1.3 million) and a cryptocurrency credit of 17,560 Won.
Fadil said initial investigations revealed the online gambling promotion was masterminded by a Canadian man residing abroad, and efforts are underway to identify him.
"The online gambling promotions targeted citizens of Japan, South Korea, and Hong Kong," he said during a press conference here yesterday.
He added that the operation is believed to have been running for the past three months and was managed by two women, who handled rentals and workers.
All suspects and seized items were taken to the Kuala Lumpur police headquarters for further action, and the case is being investigated under Section 4 (1) (e) of the Common Gaming Houses Act 1953.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Political clarity, fiscal discipline key to reviving foreign investor confidence [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia will need greater political certainty and prudent fiscal management to attract foreign investors back into the equity market, as renewed portfolio inflows in June may not yet signal a sustained recovery in sentiment, analysts said.
Tradeview Capital fund manager Neoh Jia Man said the continued outflow from Malaysian equities in June signals that foreign investor confidence remains fragile, suggesting the return to net inflows is likely a short-term rebound rather than a sustained turnaround.
He said the direction of US Federal Reserve (Fed) policy will also be a key factor shaping global capital flows into emerging markets, including Malaysia.
"A less hawkish tone under the new Fed chair would help draw more capital into our market.
"Domestically, greater clarity on the timing of the general election, along with sustained fiscal discipline from the government, would also help attract fund flows back," he told Business Times.
Neoh also noted that capital flows are influenced by a combination of domestic and external factors.
He said improving economic and fiscal fundamentals would help attract investor interest, but this has to be weighed against the yield differential offered by Malaysia relative to regional peers.
Similarly, UOB Global Economics & Markets Research said near-term capital flow prospects will remain largely driven by US Fed rate expectations, developments in US-Iran relations, evolving US trade tensions, and domestic policy continuity.
"While Malaysia's economic fundamentals remain supportive, external factors are likely to exert a greater influence on investor sentiment, particularly amid heightened caution over AI-related valuation risks.
"As such, capital flows are expected to remain volatile, with the ringgit likely to experience continued fluctuations in the coming months," it said in a macro note yesterday.
According to the bank, Malaysia's foreign portfolio flows rebounded to a net inflow of RM2.5 billion in June, reversing from a net outflow of RM8.0 billion in May.
The recovery was mainly driven by net foreign purchases of Malaysian debt securities amounting to RM4.9 billion, which more than offset continued net equity outflows of RM2.4 billion.
The improvement coincided with easing tensions in the Middle East following the US-Iran agreement to engage in 60 days of peace talks, as well as the approach of two state elections in Malaysia.
This came despite growing expectations of a US Fed interest rate hike later this year.
In line with the stronger capital inflows, Bank Negara Malaysia's international reserves rose for a third consecutive month to US$132.6 billion in June, the highest level since January 2014.
Reserve adequacy remained sound at 4.7 months of retained imports of goods and services and 0.9 times short-term external debt.
Meanwhile, Bank Negara's net short foreign exchange swap position widened for a fourth consecutive month to US$24.7 billion at the end of May, equivalent to 18.9 per cent of total reserves and the highest level since April 2025.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit opens slightly higher versus greenback amid Strait of Hormuz tension
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened slightly higher against the US dollar on Friday amid continued caution over vessel movements in the Strait of Hormuz as investors monitored geopolitical tension.
At 8am, the local currency strengthened to 4.0720/0815 against the US dollar from 4.0750/0795 at Thursday's close.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said the ringgit was also supported by yesterday's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) statement, which suggested that the Malaysian economy is on a stronger footing.
"The sharp rise in electrical and electronics exports indicates that Malaysia is benefiting from global investment in artificial intelligence and technology. This translates into greater confidence and supports the ringgit," he told Bernama.
At the opening, the ringgit was traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
It strengthened against the Japanese yen to 2.5080/5140 from 2.5091/5120 at Thursday's close, appreciated against the British pound to 5.4618/4745 from 5.4654/4714, and strengthened against the euro to 4.6555/6664 from 4.6581/6633.
Against regional currencies, the local note edged up against the Singapore dollar to 3.1512/1588 from 3.1518/1558 at Thursday's close and appreciated against the Indonesian rupiah to 224.6/225.2 from 224.8/225.1.
It slipped against the Thai baht to 12.1978/2325 from 12.1886/2064 and was almost unchanged against the Philippine peso at 6.61/6.63, compared with 6.61/6.62 previously.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US home prices hit all-time high in June
Article:
LOS ANGELES: Housing affordability challenges continue to rattle United States (US) buyers, as home prices in the country hit an all-time high last month, the National Association of Realtors (NAR) said on Thursday, reported Xinhua.
The median existing-home price climbed to a record high of US$440,600 in June, the NAR said in a news release.
Prices were up 1.8 per cent from a year ago, marking the third consecutive year of monthly home price increases, according to the trade association.
"Housing affordability is showing signs of improvement lately, but costs still may be the biggest reason many buyers remain hesitant to get into the market," said the NAR in the news release.
It added that existing-home sales – which includes single-family homes, townhomes, condos and co-ops – fell 2.4 per cent in June from the previous month, backing off a five-month high reached in May.
"The back-and-forth in monthly home sales activity, driven by mild fluctuations in mortgage rates, shows how sensitive home buyers are to affordability conditions," said Lawrence Yun, NAR's chief economist.
"However, job gains – more than half a million since the beginning of the year – will continue to provide support for the housing market."
Housing supply also remains a significant constraint.
According to the NAR, housing inventory remains at historical lows, with the number of homes for sale declining 0.6 per cent from May and rising only 1.3 per cent from a year earlier, limiting options for buyers and continuing to put upward pressure on prices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Six dead in wildfire in southern SpainS
Article:
MADRID, July 10, 2026 (AFP) -
Six people died in a wildfire near Almeria in southern Spain late Thursday, regional authorities said, adding some of the victims were found in vehicles.
The deaths took place in the hamlet of Bedar, the regional government said in a statement, saying around 150 firefighters were battling the blaze which broke out as temperatures soared.
The fire injured at least six people, including a woman who suffered burns and another person with smoke inhalation who were taken to hospital.
The four others were treated at the scene for minor burns and respiratory problems caused by heavy smoke.
Witnesses told authorities the blaze may have started after a power line fell, igniting dry vegetation before spreading rapidly through surrounding woodland.
Authorities have not confirmed the cause of the fire.
Roads were closed and residents evacuated as the fire spread, with about 50 people housed in a cultural centre.
Spain's Military Emergency Unit (UME), which is deployed in major emergencies, was due to join firefighting efforts in the coming hours.
The head of the regional government of Andalusia, Juanma Moreno, expressed his condolences to the families of the six people who died, calling it "a tragedy."
"Our hearts are heavy and we are devastated by grief," he wrote on X.
The wildfire comes as Spain suffers a heatwave, with scorching temperatures triggering orange weather warnings - the second highest level –  across parts of Andalusia in recent days.
Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez said in May that Spain would deploy its largest-ever summer wildfire response this year.
Spain has experienced increasingly frequent and prolonged heatwaves in recent years, with temperatures often exceeding 40C, creating conditions for major wildfires.
More than 393,000 hectares (971,000 acres) burned in Spain in 2025, according to the European Forest Fire Information System (EFFIS), making it the country's worst wildfire year in recent history.
mdm-ds/cms
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HTAR reassigns medical officers to address staff shortages and workloads
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hospital Tengku Ampuan Rahimah (HTAR) has reassigned medical officers to its General Surgery Department and expects additional permanent staff to join in August to help ease heavy workloads and frequent on-call duties.
In a statement today, the Selangor Health Department said the measures were introduced after staffing changes temporarily affected duty rosters, while stressing that hospital services had continued without interruption.
"Several newly appointed permanent and contract medical officers have been assigned to critical departments, including the General Surgery Department.
"Medical officers have also been redeployed between departments to help manage workloads in areas experiencing higher demand," it said.
The department said HTAR was expected to receive additional permanent medical officers in August, who would be deployed to departments requiring more manpower.
It said the General Surgery Department currently has 32 medical officers, comprising permanent and contract staff, as well as doctors undergoing specialist training.
However, staffing levels fluctuate due to transfers and new postings.
"The situation has had a temporary impact on duty roster planning, resulting in more frequent on-call duties.
"The head of department has had to reorganise work schedules to ensure services continue without disruption," it said.
The department added that the hospital would continue monitoring workloads across all departments and redeploying manpower where necessary.
"Regular engagement sessions between the head of department and representatives of the medical officers will also be held to identify the best solutions to operational issues on the ground.
"HTAR's main focus is to ensure that the quality of care, staff welfare and patient safety remain the highest priorities," it said.
On July 7, CodeBlue reported that medical officers in HTAR's General Surgery Department were grappling with heavy workloads and frequent on-call duties amid staffing shortages.
The report also highlighted concerns over doctor
fatigue
and its potential impact on patient care.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US to guide Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon zones, lead new talks
Article:
BEIRUT: The United States will oversee the withdrawal of Israeli forces from "pilot zones" in the south, with the first to get underway within days, Lebanese and US officials said Thursday.
A US official said that new talks between Israel and Lebanon would go ahead next week, after a diplomatic source earlier told AFP that Lebanon had demanded an Israeli pullout from the zones before taking part.
Under a framework agreement reached on June 26, Israel will gradually withdraw from areas of southern Lebanon where it has sent troops as part of its military campaign against Hizbollah, the Iranian-backed Shia movement that has long battled Israel.
As part of the agreement, the long-disempowered Lebanese military will take full control of two small areas dubbed pilot zones.
The US ambassador to Lebanon, Michel Issa, told President Joseph Aoun that "an American military delegation will arrive in Beirut in the coming days to... determine the mechanism for implementation on the ground," according to the Lebanese presidency.
In Washington, a US official said that "we have moved to the implementation stage."
"The first pilot zone will launch in a matter of days, and further pilot zones are being mapped out and planned," the official said on condition of anonymity.
US Central Command will coordinate on the zones with both countries, he said.
[[nid:1484925]]
"We will soon begin outreach to international partners to help the Lebanese government effectively restore sovereignty in these zones and across their country more broadly," the official said.
The agreement says that Lebanon will exert full responsibility for the zones only upon "confirmation of successful disarmament of non-state armed groups", a reference to Hizbollah, for years effectively outside the control of the Lebanese government.
The agreement – rejected by Hizbollah – does not set a timetable for Israel's withdrawal, and Israeli officials have also vowed that their forces will remain in a "security zone" 10 kilometres (six miles) deep as long as Hizbollah remains armed.
"It is essential to avoid any vacuum when Israeli forces withdraw from the designated area," Issa added, according to a statement from the Lebanese presidency.
Aoun, for his part, once again called on the United States to "exert pressure on Israel to halt military operations and comply with the provisions of the framework".
Aoun is expected to visit Washington later this month at the invitation of his American counterpart Donald Trump.
The latest Israel-Lebanon talks will take place in Rome next Wednesday and Thursday.
The US official said the talks would take place at the level of technical teams and be closed to the press. Italy had earlier announced that the talks would take place between ambassadors, much like previous rounds in Washington.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar denies claim govt lied over TAR UMT's 10-year tax exemption extension
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has denied allegations that the government misled the public over Tunku Abdul Rahman University of Management and Technology's (TAR UMT) tax exemption, insisting the institution was granted the promised 10-year extension.
He said the three-year period stated in a Finance Ministry letter referred only to the university's requirement to submit audited financial statements, in accordance with Subsection 44(6) of the Income Tax Act 1967.
"We granted a 10-year extension, which has already been announced. We only required the university to submit its audited report within three years. That is all.
"What is wrong with that? We did not lie," he said at the Yok Merahkan Johor Undi Harapan programme in Taman Pelangi Indah here yesterday.
Also present were Pakatan Harapan (PH) candidates for the Puteri Wangsa and Kempas state seats, Dr Maszlee Malik and Muhammad Faezuddin Mohd Puad.
Anwar was responding to MCA president Datuk Seri Dr Wee Ka Siong, who claimed that a Finance Ministry letter issued this month showed the tax exemption had been extended for only three years instead of the promised 10.
The prime minister reiterated that the government remained committed to ensuring access to education for all communities.
"We only asked for the audited report. If the current arrangement does not allow it, then amend the scheme. The extension period remains unchanged. This is an issue of education," he said.
Anwar added that he had initially refrained from responding publicly to avoid escalating the dispute.
On Wednesday, Madani government spokesman Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the Cabinet had reviewed the matter and approved the
10-year tax exemption extension
, recognising TAR UMT's contribution to providing quality and affordable private higher education.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Florida airport renamed 'Trump' in latest push to expand his brand
Article:
WEST PALM BEACH: A Florida airport was renamed after Donald Trump on Thursday, accelerating a push by the US president and supporters to plaster his name and image on everything from national landmarks to banknotes.
The Republican leader's son Eric celebrated by landing in the private family Boeing 757 – so-called "Trump Force One" – at what had been known as Palm Beach International Airport for nearly half a century.
"A beautiful day," a grinning Eric Trump said on Fox News at the newly minted President Donald J. Trump International Airport, a short drive from the family's Mar-a-Lago golf resort and compound.
The airport renaming was signed into law by Florida's Republican governor, Ron DeSantis.
"I don't think there's anybody more synonymous with Palm Beach than Donald Trump and maybe all of Florida," Eric Trump said.
He pointed out that the main road leading out of the airport is already called "President Donald J. Trump Boulevard".
And now anyone flying into Palm Beach will have a ticket where "you're going to see 'DJT' in perpetuity".
Travellers at the airport Thursday were divided on the name change.
Corporate pilot Chris Bailey welcomed the switch, telling AFP that Trump has "certainly done enough to warrant a name of an airport".
But some were hostile.
"Palm Beach is a better brand. People don't really like Donald Trump," John Manov, a doctor, said. "It seems in poor taste."
BANKNOTES, BUILDINGS, BRIDGE
Breaking the long-held US tradition against celebrating living figures – and particularly serving presidents – on currency and buildings, the Trump name and image are spreading rapidly.
Officials, including US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, unveiled the new Donald J. Trump Bridge in Tennessee on Thursday.
"The Tennessee license plate used to have a sign on it, or a little motto that said, 'Follow me to Tennessee,'" Bessent said. "Now, everyone who follows those cars is going to go across the Donald J. Trump Bridge on the I-40."
Washington already features the renamed Donald J. Trump United States Institute of Peace and giant posters with his face have been hung from the facades of major government buildings.
Trump's name was also briefly imposed on the famous Kennedy Center performing arts venue in the capital, before a judge ruled this illegal.
In addition, there are Trump-branded visa, medical and other official government programmes.
His image is due to appear on some US passports and already appears on passes to National Parks. An entire "Trump class" of naval ships has been announced.
In yet another first, Trump's signature will appear on US dollar notes later this year, the Treasury says.
Critics liken the spread of the Trump brand to cults of personality usually seen in authoritarian countries and dictatorships.
Eric Trump denied there was anything wrong with the airport renaming, telling Fox News that "anything that bears our name is slightly controversial".
Asked about how the president feels to have the Florida airport named after him, Eric Trump said he was "honoured" and "humbled".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Burnham on track to be UK PM as scores of Labour MPs back leadership bid
Article:
LONDON: Veteran politician Andy Burnham was on track Thursday to become Britain's next prime minister as hundreds of Labour lawmakers formally nominated him to succeed Keir Starmer as party leader.
The 56-year-old is the only member of parliament from the UK's ruling party to publicly declare themselves a candidate to replace Starmer, who announced he was quitting last month.
Burnham appeared on course to be crowned Labour leader unchallenged after the party announced that 322 of its 403 MPs had backed him on the first day of nominations on Thursday.
"It is all starting to feel very real," Burnham said in a social media video posted shortly after the process opened.
With 322 nominations, he seemed on an unstoppable path to the Labour leadership and thus the premiership, as there is very little chance for another challenger to get the 81 signatures required to join the race.
Nominations close on July 16. In the absence of a contest, Burnham will be crowned Labour leader – and prime minister in waiting – at a special conference the following day.
He would then replace Starmer at 10 Downing Street on July 20 after meeting King Charles III, becoming Britain's seventh prime minister in a decade.
"There's no one else," one Labour MP told AFP on condition of anonymity after nominating Burnham.
In a post on Facebook, Burnham said he was "deeply grateful" for the across-the-party support from Labour MPs "who have put their trust in me."
"That is the circuit breaker I am offering: power out of Westminster, an economy rewired for ordinary people, and good growth in every postcode."
Burnham, nicknamed the "King of the North" for winning three consecutive Greater Manchester mayoral elections, has vowed to "bring about the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen."
His signature proposal is the creation of a "No. 10 North" to coordinate greater devolution – a reference to the UK prime minister's address at 10 Downing Street.
Burnham has pledged fiscal discipline and to reduce the country's ballooning welfare bill, having already sought to calm markets by committing to the government's current borrowing limits.
But he will face the same challenges that buffeted Starmer's almost two-year premiership, causing him to resign last month – notably anaemic growth, a cost-of-living squeeze and an unpredictable US president in Donald Trump.
He has also indicated he could stake out a different path to Starmer on Israel, which enjoyed solid backing from the Labour government even as criticism grew of its war in Gaza.
Starmer, under pressure for months over policy U-turns and questions about his judgement, announced on June 22 that he was resigning after losing the support of Labour MPs.
His move came after Burnham won a by-election that allowed him to return to parliament to launch a widely expected leadership challenge.
On the day Starmer announced his resignation, Burnham was sworn into parliament, becoming an MP again following his stint between 2001 and 2017.
BREATH OF FRESH AIR
Burnham – seen as slightly to the left of the more centrist Starmer, and more charismatic – is Labour's most popular politician, surveys show.
Many MPs feel he is the party's best chance of clawing back support from Nigel Farage's anti-immigrant Reform UK party before the next general election, expected in 2029.
Reform has led Labour in national opinion polls for well over a year, although the gap has narrowed in recent weeks amid questions over Farage's finances.
One Labour MP, who asked not to be named, said the party was right to "roll the dice" on Burnham, saying "he couldn't be worse than Starmer."
"I hope he's a breath of fresh air," the lawmaker told AFP.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: BTH: The child, the crime & the system [WATCH]
Article:
A SCHOOL violence incident can leave a community searching for answers — raising difficult questions about safety, mental health, disability rights and how we respond when children are involved in moments of crisis.
In this episode of Beyond the Headlines, we look beyond the headlines to examine the wider systems surrounding children; How do we balance accountability with protection? Are we doing enough to understand the needs of children facing emotional, behavioural or developmental challenges? And how can schools, families and institutions create environments that are safer and more inclusive?
Joining hosts Amalina Kamal and Elena Koshy are clinical psychologist Prashanth Vasantha Kumar and lawyer Chrishanthini Sebastiampillai.
The episode looks at the responsibilities surrounding the sharing of content involving minors, the importance of safeguarding children's dignity, and why building a safer society requires collaboration between legal institutions, educators, mental health professionals, families and communities.
This discussion does not address the facts, individuals, evidence or legal proceedings of any ongoing case. Instead, it focuses on broader issues surrounding child protection, disability rights, mental health awareness, trauma support and school safety.
More in the latest episode of Beyond the Headlines on NST's Online's YouTube channel.
* This episode was recorded on July 9
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US-Iran strikes: Latest developments
Article:
DUBAI: The United States struck scores of targets in Iran, sparking retaliation from Teheran on American allies in the Gulf.
Here are the latest developments:
Trump speaks to Netanyahu
US President Donald Trump updated Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu about Washington's latest "moves in the Gulf," the latter's office said.
"The continued coordination between the countries in various sectors was established," Netanyahu's office said in a statement on X.
Iran says defence HQ hit
Iranian state media reported that a US-Israeli projectile hit a military headquarters on the outskirts of Bushehr, where its only civilian nuclear plant is located.
A US defence official, however, said that Washington was not currently carrying out any strikes on Iran.
U
S says no major damage
Dozens of missiles and drones fired by Iran caused no significant damage or injuries to American personnel, a US defence official said.
They "were intercepted or failed to cause major damage," the official said on condition of anonymity, adding that there were "no US injuries".
Explosions were heard over the Bahraini capital of Manama on Thursday morning and air raid sirens sounded for a third time after Iran's military said it had targeted sites in the Gulf kingdom as well as in Kuwait and Qatar.
[[nid:1484903]]
Earlier, Iran's Revolutionary Guards said they had struck "key infrastructure and facilities" at US bases in Arifjan and Ali Al Salem in Kuwait, and Juffair and Sheikh Isa in Bahrain.
Israel 'ready to attack Iran'
Defence Minister Israel Katz said Israel was prepared to resume its military campaign against Iran if needed, vowing to do so "with even greater force".
"The army is ready and on alert for a resumption of fighting, in order to regain air superiority and strike again... in Iran, to eliminate threats, including a third time if necessary," Katz said at a military ceremony.
Crowds mass for Khamenei burial
Thousands of people filled the streets of Mashhad – many waving the red flags that symbolise the search for vengeance in Shia Islam – as Iran's late supreme leader Ali Khamenei's body was transported there for burial.
The burial will be the final act of a multi-day funeral, after he lay in state in Teheran before being processed through the Iranian capital and the holy Iraqi cities of Najaf and Karbala.
[[nid:1484902]]
Markets positive
Global stock markets were largely positive as investors rediscovered their appetite for tech names and brushed off a new volley of strikes between the United States and Iran, with oil prices falling slightly.
In New York, the main indexes closed higher, with the tech-rich Nasdaq Composite jumping 1.3 per cent.
Jordan intercepts missiles
The Jordanian military intercepted Iranian missiles which triggered warning sirens, government spokesman Mohammad Momani told the state-run channel Al-Mamlaka on Thursday.
A senior military source at the General Command of the Jordanian Armed Forces said in a later statement that eight missiles were intercepted in total, causing no casualties or damage.
17 killed in US strikes
Two days of US strikes against Iran have killed 14 people and injured 78, Teheran's health ministry said on Thursday.
Separately, Ahmad Nafisi, deputy provincial governor of southern province of Hormozgan, said Thursday that US strikes in the fishing port town of Sirik killed three people and injured 15 more, state TV reported.
Teheran-Mashhad railway suspended
The train service between the capital Teheran and Mashhad has been suspended after US strikes, state TV reported, hours before Khamenei's burial in the holy city.
The Islamic Republic of Iran Railways blamed "a criminal attack by the US-Israeli enemy" on the route and said it had dispatched repair teams, the state broadcaster reported.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Charlie Kirk killing: Suspect in tears after killing, says roommate
Article:
LOS ANGELES: The man accused of murdering influential right-wing campaigner Charlie Kirk broke down in tears of regret days after the killing, according to video testimony played in a US court on Thursday.
The development came on the fourth day of a hearing to determine if there is enough evidence to try 23-year-old Tyler Robinson for the university campus assassination of the influential Kirk, a supporter of Donald Trump who was credited with mobilising a huge youth vote for the 80-year-old Republican president.
"I just asked him in person if what he said was true the night before, and he said it was," Robinson's roommate Lance Twiggs told investigators in the taped interview.
"He started crying a little bit, and said he wishes he hadn't done it."
Robinson, who was raised a Republican, faces the death penalty if he is found guilty of shooting Kirk on a Utah campus last September, in a crime that sent shock waves through the United States and roiled right-wing politics.
Investigators earlier revealed that Robinson had confessed to the murder via text messages to Twiggs, in which he indicated he was fed up with the "hatred" Kirk spread.
[[nid:1484872]]
His relationship with Twiggs has been the subject of fascination in the US, where the case is being closely followed.
Twiggs, who is transitioning to become a woman, was not merely a roommate but was also in a romantic relationship with Robinson, with fevered online speculation that this may have provided a motive for the murder of Kirk, a Christian nationalist who was highly critical of the LGBT community and transgender people.
Speaking to investigators, Twiggs acknowledged that Robinson sometimes discussed politics.
"He usually talked about stuff he heard on the radio on his drive to work," the witness recounted. But prior to the murder, "I personally had never heard him talk about Charlie Kirk before specifically."
Twiggs also said the couple had "not really" discussed the situation of transgender people and the LGBT community in the United States.
They first met in 2023 – initially just as roommates – before beginning a romantic relationship "two or three months" after Robinson moved in, Twiggs told investigators.
Robinson has not yet indicated how he intends to plead.
This current set of proceedings, in Utah, is expected to finish on Friday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US holds off new tariffs on commercial aircraft imports
Article:
WASHINGTON: The United States has refrained from imposing new tariffs on commercial aircraft and parts after an investigation into the sector, according to a proclamation signed Thursday by President Donald Trump.
The Commerce Department's probe found that actions of foreign countries "continue to harm" the US commercial aircraft manufacturing industry and that the United States is too reliant on foreign supply chains.
But the Commerce secretary "also recommended that no immediate tariffs be imposed" to address the potential national security risks posed by imports of commercial aircraft, jet engines and associated parts, the proclamation said.
Since returning to the White House last year, Trump has imposed sweeping tariffs on US trading partners and sharp duties targeting sectors like steel, aluminium and automobiles.
While many of Trump's global tariffs were eventually struck down by the Supreme Court, his sector-specific duties remain in place.
The aircraft investigation came under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which is the same authority Trump used to roll out other sectoral tariffs.
But for now, the Trump administration is seeking further negotiations with trading partners to "adjust" imports such that their volumes "will not threaten to impair the national security of the United States".
Trump's proclamation nonetheless keeps the door open to the possibility of further action down the line.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump updated Netanyahu on US 'moves in Gulf'
Article:
JERUSALEM: US President Donald Trump briefed Benjamin Netanyahu on Thursday about US operations in the Gulf, the Israeli Prime Minister's office said, after Iran reported fresh American strikes.
"As part of the continuous contact" between the two, "President Trump updated the Prime Minister on American moves in the Gulf," Netanyahu's office said in a message on X.
A US official confirmed the call occurred without providing further details Thursday.
The Israeli leader also voiced concern to the White House about Turkiye, accusing "Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and his people" of making statements "against the existence of the State of Israel, as well as the need for security zones along Israel's borders," according to the post on X.
Erdogan on Tuesday rejected as "disinformation" Israel's claim that the sale to Ankara of US F-35 fighter jets and components would upset the regional power balance.
On Monday, Netanyahu urged the United States not to sell the jets to Nato ally Turkiye.
Tensions have mounted between Trump and Netanyahu over the handling of the war with Iran, which has cost the US president politically with prices of oil and consumer goods surging during the conflict.
The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday, based on anonymous sources, that Israel also told the US that its intelligence had detected a new Iranian plot to kill Trump.
Iran has openly vowed to retaliate against Trump since his first term, when the US assassinated Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps general Qassem Suleimani.
Teheran's calls for retaliation have only sharpened since the US-Israel joint attacks that began on Feb 28, killing Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Former Iranian supreme leader buried
Article:
MASHHAD: Iran on Friday laid to rest its former supreme leader Ali Khamenei over four months after his killing in an airstrike, as two days of US and Iranian attacks raised fears of a return to all-out war.
Khamenei's flag-covered coffin was carried aloft into the shrine of Imam Reza in his home city of Mashhad in eastern Iran as a sea of people waited outside and listened to prayers, with no sign of a public appearance by his son and successor Mojtaba Khamenei.
The "body of the martyred leader of the Islamic Revolution was buried in the memorial hall of the shrine of Imam Reza," state broadcaster IRIB reported.
The burial came after a second day of tit-for-tat attacks by Washington and Teheran's forces, with Iranian officials reporting that US strikes killed 17 people and state media saying one targeted a railway line between Teheran and Mashhad.
The Islamic republic said it had resumed attacks targeting US assets in Kuwait, Bahrain and Qatar, while sirens blared in Jordan – another American ally – where the military said it had intercepted eight missiles launched from Iran.
Khamenei was killed alongside close family members on the first day of the US-Israeli war against Iran on February 28.
The last of a marathon six days of funeral ceremonies, Khamenei's burial in Mashhad was closely watched for signs of Mojtaba Khamenei, who has yet to appear in public since being appointed.
Parliament speaker and chief negotiator in talks with the United States, Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, powerful chief justice Gholamhossein Mohseni Ejei, and Khamenei's eldest son Mostafa Khamenei were all present at the shrine, with some senior figures weeping over Khamenei's coffin, state TV images showed.
But as on other days of the funeral process, there was no sign of Mojtaba Khamenei, who since being named has only communicated through written statements and is said to have been wounded in the February 28 strikes.
[[nid:1484957]]
CROWDS CALL FOR VENGEANCE
Crowds of men and women clad in black, many urging vengeance against US President Donald Trump, had massed in Mashhad against the backdrop of slogans including: "Hey Trump we will k*** you," written in English, AFP correspondents said.
The Wall Street Journal meanwhile reported Thursday, based on anonymous sources, that Israel told the United States that their intelligence had detected a new Iranian plot to kill Trump.
The United States on Thursday targeted the perimeter of Iran's only civilian nuclear plant with a strike, Iranian state media reported, citing the deputy governor of Bushehr province.
State media also reported Thursday that a projectile hit a military headquarters on the outskirts of Bushehr, but a US defence official said the US military was not currently carrying out strikes on Iran.
State media said the United States targeted a section of the Teheran-Mashhad railway 55 kilometres (34 miles) from Mashhad, forcing the closure of the line. Passengers were transferred to buses.
Iran's army said it had targeted a Patriot missile system in Kuwait, an early warning system in Qatar and fuel tanks in Bahrain with drones, as part of its attacks on US bases in the region.
But a US defence official said the dozens of missiles and drones fired by Iran were either intercepted or caused no significant damage, with no injuries to American personnel.
In a sign of the tense environment, at least one fighter jet escorted the plane carrying Khamenei's coffin to its final burial in Mashhad, footage from the supreme leader's website showed.
[[nid:1484956]]
THIRD ATTACK ON IRAN?
Following Israel's June 2025 war against Iran and this year's conflict, Defence Minister Israel Katz warned at a military ceremony on Thursday that Israel was prepared to attack Iran for a "third time" if needed, vowing to do so "with even greater force".
"We are preparing for every scenario," added Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, whose office said he spoke with Trump on Thursday, with the US president providing an update on "American moves in the Gulf".
The Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil and gas corridor, remains a key point of contention between the two sides, with Teheran insisting on control of the waterway despite it being open to free passage before the war began.
The Iranian military hit at least three ships in recent days, prompting extensive US strikes against Iranian targets from Tuesday.
Traffic through the strait has fallen sharply since Wednesday, especially through the UN-backed Omani route, according to data from maritime tracking firm Kpler, after vessels were attacked earlier this week.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Federal grand jury indicts eight men over alleged plot to attack White House UFC event
Article:
WASHINGTON: A federal grand jury has indicted eight men over an alleged plot to attack the Ultimate Fighting Championship mixed martial arts event on the White House lawn in June, the US Department of Justice said in a statement on Thursday.
Here are the details:
1.The FBI said last month it thwarted a planned attack on the White House UFC event.
2.All eight men were charged together on Thursday in a two-count indictment returned in Columbus, Ohio. The first arrest took place in Ohio. The Justice Department said the remaining defendants have also since been arrested.
3.They were aged from 19 to 32.
4.The indictment charged all of them in two conspiracies: "conspiracy to provide material support to terrorists" and "conspiracy to commit murder on federal government territory and to murder a federal government official," the Justice Department said.
5.The defendants allegedly planned to murder President Donald Trump, Vice President JD Vance, US officials, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, billionaire Elon Musk and "other high value targets" at the event, the DOJ said.
6.Trump attended the event, which was staged without incident on his 80th birthday, along with several senior Republican lawmakers, donors and administration officials to commemorate the 250th anniversary of US independence.
7.Netanyahu did not attend the event.
8.The defendants could not be reached for comment.
9.The eight men allegedly participated in online chat groups and forums on Signal, SimpleX, Discord, TikTok and Instagram, the DOJ said.
10.The plot involved using explosive-laden drones to strike the north side of the White House with the aim of funnelling attendees towards an exit where snipers planned to open fire on fleeing politicians and others, the FBI alleged last month.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: BN, PH rivalry takes centre stage
Article:
JOHOR BARU: If the Johor election campaign were to be judged solely by the battle of posters and flags, it would paint a mixed picture of the contests across the 56 state seats.
While some areas, particularly Felda settlements, have witnessed an intense display of campaign posters and party flags, others, especially urban constituencies, have seen noticeably fewer of these, perhaps reflecting the unique way people here celebrate democracy.
In reality, the campaign has been anything but subdued. The fiercest battle has been between Barisan Nasional and Pakatan Harapan, allies at the federal level but political rivals in the state election.
The rivalry between the two coalitions has been so intense that it has overshadowed the infighting within Perikatan Nasional and reduced Parti Bersama Malaysia, which is making its election debut, as well as the other contesting parties and independent candidates to little more than a sideshow.
Over the past 13 days, the  political temperature in the state has been rising,  with BN and PH  engaging in a fierce war of narratives and both sides attempting to define the issues that matter most to voters.
One of the major issues that has highlighted this rivalry is the Johor-Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ) master plan, expected to be launched during the Malaysia-Singapore Leaders' Retreat in the fourth quarter of this year.
The  spotlight also turned to Johor BN chairman Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi when he faced pressure to participate in a televised debate with former education minister Dr Maszlee Malik.
The debate proceeded without Onn Hafiz's participation, and with BN leaders defending the decision by pointing out that the two politicians are contesting different seats.
PH has gone on the offensive in its campaign, seeking to woo support, repeat its success in wresting control of Johor in the 2018 election, or prevent BN from securing another landslide victory.
Apart from criticising the shortcomings of Onn Hafiz and his state administration, several PH leaders have also sought to link the state election to efforts to secure the release of imprisoned former BN chairman Datuk Seri Najib Razak.
Observers view the move as an attempt to sway support among Chinese voters and middle-class Malay voters in favour of PH.
In the last 24 hours, leaders from all parties, including those from Perikatan Nasional, have intensified their efforts to make their case to voters and highlight their aspirations for Johor, while also refuting attacks from their rivals.
Onn Hafiz's message was clear, with the caretaker Johor menteri besar urging voters to use their ballots tomorrow to reject the opposition, which he accused of making unfulfilled promises in the past.
During the same period, Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, who was here during last weekend, has also been making his final rounds in the state, attending three ceramahs yesterday  to lend support to PH candidates contesting Senggarang, Rengit and Puteri Wangsa.
Anwar is also scheduled to continue campaigning for PH candidates in the remaining hours before the campaign period ends at 11.59pm today.
On the PN side, however, the coalition is seen struggling to establish a consistent narrative among its leaders. For instance, Bersatu president Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin recently conceded that PN may not be able to form the next state government after  polling day tomorrow.
However, Bersatu secretary-general Datuk Seri Azmin Ali appeared to strike a different tone when he said PN could still form the next state government with support from Muda. This came before Muda president Amira Aisya Abdul Aziz was quoted as ruling out any form of cooperation with PN.
Since Johor is considered a good bellwether state due to its mix of urban and rural areas as well as its diverse population, a political analyst said the outcome of the polls would be important for BN and PH in measuring their level of support and acceptance among the people.
Senior lecturer at Universiti Malaya's Department of Political Science, Public Administration and Development Studies, Dr Mohammad Tawfik Yaakub, said the state is important for BN as it serves as a platform to reaffirm its position as the dominant political force in Johor.
"A strong BN victory would strengthen the menteri besar's mandate, boost confidence in the state government's policies and signal that BN is beginning to regain momentum and build a strong political foundation ahead of the 16th General Election.
"For PH, the state election is significant as it tests the extent to which its support remains intact in a diverse state such as Johor.
"Although PH is part of the unity government, it still needs to prove in Johor that it remains relevant, competitive and capable of offering convincing policies to urban, semi-urban, young and middle-class voters," he said.
A PH election machinery worker said the state election goes beyond Johor but that off the national scale.
He said a landslide victory for BN would not only give it control of the state but could also pro-vide the coalition with greater bargaining power at the federal level.
"When a party becomes too dominant, its bargaining power increases.
"And when bargaining power increases, pressure on the federal government also grows.
"One state could change the country's entire political landscape."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt support steadies SMEs amid global tensions
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Swift government intervention helped small and medium enterprises (SMEs) to weather the global energy crisis and geopolitical uncertainty, said the Small and Medium Enterprises Association of Malaysia (Samenta) yesterday.
Samenta national president Datuk William Ng said timely fiscal planning, supply chain management and targeted financial aid prevented widespread business closures as geopolitical tensions escalated.
He said SMEs had prepared for a worst-case scenario, including Brent crude rising above US$100 a barrel, higher inflation and supply disruptions linked to tensions involving  US attacks on Iran.
"Five months into the crisis, the expected economic paralysis has not materialised.
"While Brent crude rose 27 per cent this year to average US$93 per barrel, Malaysia's economy defied global trends by posting  gross domestic product  growth of 5.4 per cent in the first quarter of 2026."
Ng said the retail sector and domestic consumption remained resilient, and Malaysia avoided business closures and job losses seen in some neighbouring countries.
He said the association attributed the soft landing to the government's foresight in maintaining petrol subsidies and enforcing anti-profiteering measures, helping keep inflation below two per cent.
"These measures insulated our SMEs from rising overhead costs. Early fuel stockpiling and coordination at major ports also prevented a supply chain crisis.
"This reinforced confidence among foreign and domestic investors."
Ng said these measures have placed Malaysia in a favourable position in Asean.
The country is among the least affected by global uncertainty and is benefiting as a safe haven for investors, he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: EU moves closer to restricting kids' use of social media
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
BRUSSELS: Restrictions on the use of social media by children in the European Union could come as early as this summer, as a long-awaited expert report next week is expected to make recommendations.
After Australia became the first country in the world to ban under-16s from social media, several EU nations, including Denmark and Greece, demanded a similar move.
The EU says all options are on the table, from a blanket ban on children from social media platforms to restrictions on certain services and features.
It appears there is little appetite for a broad-brush approach and EU officials insist no decisions have been taken before the panel tasked by EU chief Ursula von der Leyen delivers its recommendations on July 13.
Von der Leyen has indicated in the past she supports restrictions, with a formal announcement expected in September — although that could change.
"It is not the question when children or teenagers would have access to social media, I would say it's more the question when social media has access to our children and teenagers," she said.
She is under pressure as some countries, including France, have already drawn up their own legislation, although Brussels on Monday told France to amend its draft law since it encroached on the European Commission's powers.
The EU says it will do more to protect children online, not just on social media.
"Whatever decisions are made on age limits, we must also tackle the business models and design choices shaping children's online experiences every day," EU consumer protection commissioner Michael McGrath said.
The panel is not expected to recommend a blanket social media ban either.
For an idea on what to expect, observers point to a German panel that put forward two options last month: a statutory minimum age of 13 — which many platforms have — or restrictions on individual services and features.
The European Commission, the EU's digital watchdog, has been watching how the ban has unfolded in Australia, where there have been challenges, and could opt for a different approach.
A majority of Europeans surveyed in France, Germany, Italy, Poland and Spain for a YouGov poll published yesterday want platforms to remove "harmful" design features.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Rope in used car dealers to boost EV infrastructure, says group
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: The Federation of Motor and Credit Companies Associations of Malaysia (FMCCAM) has lauded the government's ambitious target of installing 30,000 electric vehicle charging stations by 2030.
However, it is calling for used car dealers to be formally integrated into the national rollout.
FMCCAM president Datuk Tony Khor Chong Boon said the country's network of used car dealerships represented an opportunity to boost EV infrastructure through stronger government-industry collaboration.
He said most of these dealers lacked specific incentives to install charging points, a gap that could help address buyers' "range anxiety" concerns if resolved.
With more than 5,000 member companies,  Khor said providing installation grants, tax incentives or soft financing could turn these dealerships into vital hubs for the national grid.
"These premises could evolve into full-scale EV service centres, offering a one-stop solution that includes sales, battery health checks, charging and dedicated after-sales support."
While welcoming the 30,000-station target, he said the success of the transition depended on more than just the number of plugs available.
He said current infrastructure was inadequate to support EV market growth, especially in secondary cities, rural areas, the east coast, as well as  Sabah and Sarawak.
"The real objective should be to build a comprehensive, sustainable and consumer-friendly ecosystem that gives Malaysians the confidence to embrace EV.
"For consumers, the biggest concern is whether charging is convenient, reliable and readily available."
On the proposed 10 per cent excise duty on EVs, he warned that the move could drive up prices of new models, which could in turn affect the resale value of used EVs.
Khor said taxation was only one factor in the valuation equation, noting that the pre-owned EV market was also heavily influenced by battery longevity, replacement costs and warranty coverage.
"A balanced policy framework that supports industry growth while protecting the used EV market is needed. Without a stable resale market, the EV transition could stall."
To address this, he proposed creating a national battery health certification and a used EV valuation standard.
"Buyers need data on the battery's life and accident records to make an informed decision."
He said Malaysia's EV strategy must encompass the whole value chain, from local manufacturing and component supply to battery recycling and digital certification systems.
"Only then can Malaysia position itself as Asean's leading EV hub."
It was reported on Wednesday that the government is adjusting its policies to achieve a target of 30,000 charging stations by 2030 in keeping pace with the growing number of EVs on roads.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cost pressures see young putting off marriage
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: Economic pressures, job mismatches and career challenges are driving young people to delay marriage, according to the National Population and Family Development Board (LPPKN).
Research, Population and Family Division director Adzmel Mahmud said the youth needed continuous support from the government, employers, educational institutions and families to help them realise their aspirations of building a family.
He said the trend required urgent attention as delayed marriages could have long-term implications for the country's population structure.
Based on Statistics Department data, the average marriage age in 2024 was 31 for men and 29 for women, a clear increase from previous decades.
"This has resulted in a shorter reproductive period that could affect birth rates in the future," he said during the second season of the Kasih Lensa Keluarga podcast held to mark World Population Day 2026.
This year's theme is "Realising the hopes and aspirations of young people — today and for the future".
Adzmel said many young people still want to marry, but financial constraints and the desire for job and income stability have compelled them to postpone their plans.
Mental, physical and social well-being also played an important role in such big decisions, he added.
On employment opportunities, he said a key challenge was the mismatch between academic qualifications and labour market demands, making it harder for many to secure the jobs and income they expect.
Meanwhile, Adzmel said investing in youth development was vital given that young Malaysians aged 15 to 30 numbered around nine million, accounting for 26 per cent of the population.
"Young people are the nation's future leaders and next generation of decision-makers. They need access to quality education, good jobs, good health and the space to make decisions and contribute to society."
Adzmel said LPPKN offered various initiatives to help young people prepare for family life,  including the Smart Start pre-marriage programme, fertility treatment assistance programme, reproductive health education, as well as family and marriage counselling services.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump updated Netanyahu on US 'moves in Gulf': Israeli PM's office
Article:
JERUSALEM: US President Donald Trump briefed Benjamin Netanyahu on Thursday about US operations in the Gulf, the Israeli prime minister's office said, after Iran reported fresh American strikes.
"As part of the continuous contact" between the two, "President Trump updated the prime minister on American moves in the Gulf," Netanyahu's office said in a post on X.
A US official confirmed the call took place but did not provide further details.
The Israeli leader also voiced concern to the White House about Turkey, accusing "Turkish President Recep Tayyip Erdogan and his people" of making statements "against the existence of the State of Israel, as well as the need for security zones along Israel's borders."
Erdogan on Tuesday rejected as "disinformation" Israel's claim that the sale to Ankara of US F-35 fighter jets and components would upset the regional balance of power.
On Monday, Netanyahu urged the United States not to sell the jets to NATO ally Turkey.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar denies buying new BMW for personal use, clarifies car is govt property
Article:
BATU PAHAT, July 10 — Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim yesterday dismissed claims that he had purchased a luxury BMW vehicle for his personal use.
“People are claiming that Anwar talks about helping the poor but has bought a new BMW. I did not buy it.
“It is an old vehicle. Why spread lies?” he said during the ‘Yok! Meriahkan Johor! Undi Harapan’ programme in Senggarang here yesterday, held in conjunction with campaigning for the 16th Johor state election.
Anwar also clarified that the Mercedes-Benz vehicle he currently uses was a gift from His Majesty Sultan Ibrahim, King of Malaysia, and had been registered under the Prime Minister’s Department rather than under his personal ownership.
“The vehicle I am using now was bestowed by the King. I did not take ownership of it personally. I registered it under the Prime Minister’s Department. I am not like others who siphon off and steal the people’s money,” he said.
The denial followed claims circulating on social media regarding the ownership status of the BMW vehicle previously used by the prime minister.
The Prime Minister’s Department (JPM) and the Ceremonial and International Conference Secretariat Division (BIUPA) said in a statement on Monday that the BMW vehicle used by the prime minister recently was an existing government asset and not a newly acquired official vehicle.
The statement said the vehicle was an asset under BIUPA’s management and designated for official government logistics.
Also present at the event were Pakatan Harapan (PH) communications director Datuk Fahmi Fadzil, who is also Communications Minister, and the coalition’s candidates for the state election in the Senggarang, Semerah and Penggaram seats, Onn Abu Bakar, Mohd Khuzzan Abu Bakar and Felicia Poh Rui Ling, respectively.
Polling takes place this Saturday, with PH contesting all 56 state seats. — Bernama

Title: Pakatan chairman Anwar fires back in Johor, scoffing at political attacks branding him a pliant ‘Chinese tool’
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, July 10 — Pakatan Harapan chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim criticised the coalition’s political detractors for alleging that he is being manipulated by the Chinese community, especially those aligned with DAP.
He said the criticisms included being a ‘tool’ for the Chinese and yielding easily when it comes to demands from the community.
“Tell me, what have I done wrong? Is it offering better education or Sara (Sumbangan Asas Rahmah financial aid) to the Chinese community?
“Or is it due to my friendship with the Chinese from DAP?
“For me, it’s better and more virtuous to have Chinese from DAP as friends than those from another Chinese party,” he said in a highly charged speech at a PH grand finale event held in conjunction with the Johor state election in Taman Pelangi Indah here late last night.
However, Anwar, who is the prime minister, stopped short of naming the Chinese-based political party that is believed to be a component of the Barisan Nasional (BN) coalition.
Present were PH leaders from the coalition’s component parties. They were also joined by PH candidates Maszlee Malik (Puteri Wangsa) and Muhammad Faezuddin Puad (Kempas).
The event last night, attended by about 1,000 people, was the third of three state election campaign programmes attended by Anwar throughout Johor yesterday.
Earlier, Anwar said the PH government had done its best to address the Chinese community’s issues, especially the long-awaited federal recognition of the Unified Examination Certificate (UEC).
“Let’s take the UEC, for many years the MCA had struggled with the issue.
“In the end who resolved the UEC issue? Not MCA, but we in the (PH) government,” said the PKR president.
Anwar also brought up Universiti Tunku Abdul Rahman’s (UTAR) RM83 million tax bill and penalties which were blamed on the PH government despite the issue being absolved.
He said a particular party in BN had unfairly faulted the government for failing to honour the UTAR issue in the past.
“They have the same access as us. Why didn’t they ask the deputy prime minister (Datuk Seri Ahmad Zahid Hamidi), who is also the BN chairman to amicably look into this?
“Why do they insist on publicly attacking us in such a manner?” he questioned, adding that the issue was also unfairly being manipulated as a political campaign by the party.
Anwar said the government had worked hard to resolve and settle the UTAR issue that was prioritised due to its link to the Chinese community’s education.
He said this was the first time that he had openly talked about such challenges faced by him and the government.
“Prior to tonight, I had deliberately avoided commenting on other parties in the unity government to avoid any heated confrontations,” he said.
Pakatan Harapan chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim with deputy chairman Mohamad Sabu at the Johor state election event in Taman Pelangi Indah, Johor Bahru last night. — Picture by Ben Tan
Anwar is expected to make another visit to Johor later today in support of the PH candidates who will be contesting in this weekend’s state poll.
PH is contesting in all 56 state seats, with 20 candidates from PKR, 19 from Parti Amanah Negara and 17 from DAP.
A total of 172 candidates are contesting in the election with polling day on Saturday.

Title: PAS bristles at Onn Hafiz’s rejection, insists Johor support for BN is no weakness
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, July 10 — Johor PAS described Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi’s rejection of the Islamist party’s offer to assist the state government should Barisan Nasional (BN) retain power after the state election as conceited and arrogant.
Johor PAS commissioner Datuk Mahfodz Mohamed said that the party’s intention to support BN was meant to sincerely assist the coalition if it did not obtain a simple majority to form the government after the state election.
In such a scenario, he explained that PAS and Perikatan Nasional (PN) were prepared to support BN in forming the state government for the stability and well-being of the people.
